channel.title: Architecture on More's awesome website
channel.description: Recent content in Architecture on More's awesome website

item.title: A Survey on Predictive Coding
item.author: morthan@qq.com (Morethan)
item.description:
The starting point was a video on YouTube explaining predictive coding. Its biggest selling point is this: a more biologically plausible alternative to the backpropagation algorithm.

The Backpropagation Algorithm
#

Backpropagation is the cornerstone of today’s deep learning landscape. Virtually all deep learning models rely on it for training. The core problem it solves is the credit assignment problem: if a neural network produces a wrong output, how should we adjust the parameters to improve the model’s output?

item.title: Practical LLM Training
item.author: morthan@qq.com (Morethan)
item.description:
Once you understand how a network architecture is implemented, a natural question arises: how do I actually train a model like this? This section compiles all sorts of engineering know-how that answers that very question.

Preface
#

Large model architectures come in all shapes and sizes, but if you really want to understand one, you have to get your hands dirty. There are plenty of high-quality online courses on YouTube to learn from— CS336: Language Modeling from Scratch being a prime example—so this article essentially serves as my notes on CS 336.

item.title: CTM Reading Notes
item.author: morthan@qq.com (Morethan)
item.description:
A novel brain-inspired architecture that introduces the concept of “temporality” to existing time-agnostic neuron models, giving rise to a series of brain-like emergent phenomena.

Preface
#

This paper caught my attention precisely because of the “magical” emergent phenomena it claims—introducing temporal information into neuron models results in human-like thought patterns of attention. These phenomena are not manually designed but naturally emerge during training, which is the most exciting part.
